01
Minds that made money · 05
He didn't beat the market.
He left it to open a grocery store.
India's most private billionaire bet on boring brick-and-mortar — right as everyone was sure the internet would kill it.
Meet the man who walked away →
02
Who
Radhakishan
Damani
Founder & Chairman, DMart (Avenue Supermarts). Born 1955, Bikaner.
One boring grocery chain
built a fortune from it — and he gives no interviews.
Born 12 July 1955, Bikaner, Rajasthan; Founder/Chairman, Avenue Supermarts (DMart). Net worth deliberately kept off this slide — figures vary by source. Sources: Wikipedia; Forbes India.
03
The IPO the market had written off doubled on day one.
Issue price
₹299
what you paid in
Listing price
~₹604
where it opened
≈100%+ on day one
BSE listing · 21 Mar 2017
Avenue Supermarts (DMart) issued at ₹299; listed ~₹604.40 on the BSE on 21 Mar 2017 — roughly a 100%+ premium on day one. Rounded; intraday and exchange figures vary slightly. Sources: Business Today; Chittorgarh.
05
The principle
Own the boring thing. Pay no rent to anyone — not landlords, not lenders.
Damani's playbookDMart owns most of its stores, sells for cash, carries little debt. The discipline IS the strategy.
What most people miss →
06
The bet wasn't the genius part.
Sitting through eight dull years was.
Fewer than 30 stores in his first 8 years. No glamour, no headlines — most people quit a plan like that long before it pays.
08
One question
Can you sit through the boring years your best decision will need?
The plan isn't the hard part. Doing nothing while it works is.
09
He didn't out-trade the market.
He out-sat it.
Now you can solve that with
AI on your side.

Education only. Not investment advice. Figures rounded and illustrative. Sources: Wikipedia (Damani; DMart); Business Today (21 Mar 2017); Chittorgarh IPO data; Forbes / Forbes India (Dec 2024).

Caption — Instagram post body

India's most private billionaire didn't beat the market with a clever stock pick. He opened a grocery store.

In 2002, with the dot-com mania still ringing in everyone's ears, Radhakishan Damani — already a feared stock investor — walked away from the screens and put his money into the most boring thing imaginable: a cash-only, no-frills discount grocery shop in Powai. No credit. No debt. No discounting-by-borrowing. He bought the land instead of renting it.

For eight years it looked like nothing. Fewer than 30 stores. No interviews, no books, no hot takes. Just the same plain white shirt and the same dull discipline, store after store. Then in 2017 DMart's IPO listed at around ₹604 against a ₹299 issue price — roughly double on day one — and the "dying" format quietly outlasted half the disruptors that were supposed to bury it.

Here's the part everyone skips. The edge wasn't the contrarian bet. It was the willingness to sit through eight glamour-free years without flinching. Most people copy the "concentrate and hold forever" line and skip the decade of boring competence underneath it. That's the actual moat.

He gives no interviews, so treat the "Damani wisdom" floating around with suspicion — most of it is legend, not on the record. We didn't fake a quote for this one.

Save this for the next time a slow, boring plan makes you itch to do something. Boring, repeated for long enough, compounds.

Save it. Share it. Send it to the friend who can't sit still through a flat year.

Education only. Not investment advice.

Sources: Wikipedia (Radhakishan Damani; DMart), Business Today (21 Mar 2017), Chittorgarh IPO data, Forbes / Forbes India (Dec 2024 / Oct 2024), exchange shareholding filings (Dec 2024).
